topic.what: CLASS (Celecoxib Long-term Arthritis Safety Study) trial comparing celecoxib vs ibuprofen and diclofenac for upper GI safety in osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis patients
topic.how: Large randomized controlled trial (n=8059) comparing celecoxib 400 mg bid vs ibuprofen 800 mg tid or diclofenac 75 mg bid, with upper GI ulcer complications as the primary endpoint over 6 months (initial publication) from a planned 12-15 month study
topic.why_matters: Initially reported a GI safety advantage for celecoxib, but subsequent release of full study data (12-15 months) showed the benefit did not persist, generating major controversy about selective data reporting and influencing regulatory standards for trial transparency

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in upper GI ulcer complications with celecoxib vs traditional NSAIDs
characteristics.value: Initial 6-month data showed lower ulcer complication rate; full 12-15-month data showed no statistically significant difference
characteristics.vs_baseline: Compared to ibuprofen 800 mg tid and diclofenac 75 mg bid
characteristics.context: Controversy over selective reporting of 6-month vs full-duration data; aspirin users included, which confounded GI outcomes

good_for: Understanding the contested evidence for COX-2 inhibitor GI safety
Case study in clinical trial transparency and selective data reporting
not_for: Definitive GI safety claims for celecoxib
Cardiovascular risk assessment (not designed for CV endpoints)
Autoimmune gastritis or H. pylori-related disease
requires: Awareness of the data reporting controversy to interpret results correctly
Understanding that concurrent aspirin use negated the GI safety advantage in subgroup analyses

novelty: One of the first large-scale RCTs designed to test GI safety of a COX-2-selective inhibitor against two traditional NSAIDs simultaneously
Became a landmark case in publication ethics and trial data transparency after full data contradicted initial report
Demonstrated that concurrent low-dose aspirin use eliminates the GI advantage of COX-2 selectivity
open_problems: Whether celecoxib at standard doses (200 mg bid) rather than the supratherapeutic study dose offers a real GI safety margin
How to interpret truncated trial publications in the context of full study data
Persistent uncertainty about the net benefit of COX-2 inhibitors when aspirin co-therapy is required
